MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION .
Off Calendar Aug-22-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant/Cross-complainant Doe's ("Doe) motion for summary adjudication is denied. Movant failed to maintain her burden of production. (CCP 437c(p).)

Doe as "Client" entered into an Attorney Representation Agreement with plaintiffs ("Attorneys"). This agreement provides:

If ATTORNEYS represent CLIENT on an individual basis only and ATTORNEYS obtain a monetary recovery for CLIENT on an individual basis, either by settlement or judgment, ATTORNEYS will be entitled to their costs related solely to CLIENT's individual representation, plus compensation for their services related solely to CLIENT's individual representation in the amount of thirty-five percent (35%) of the total amount of the Gross Value of the settlement or award if obtained before trial begins or the amount of forty percent (40%) if the case is resolved after trial commences or goes to judgment. This limitation is not applicable if the attorneys' fee is negotiated separately or paid separately so as not to reduce the gross value of CLIENT's recovery. "Gross Value" means the total of all monetary awards obtained whether by settlement, mediated result, arbitration award or court judgment including back and front pay, and damages, but excluding "expenses."

(Hiller Decl., Plaintiff's Complaint, Ex. A (emphasis added).)

Doe contends that under this agreement, "?Attorneys are entitled to only 35% of the total settlement amount with Bank One identified in the settlement agreement with Bank One, plus their costs incurred in the case solely related to their individual representation of DOE?That DOE is entitled to the remainder of the settlement amount with Bank One as identified in the settlement agreement, after Attorney's 35% contingency and their costs are deducted." (Doe's Points & Authorities, 15:24-16:2.)

Doe's undisputed material fact four further asserts "Doe settled her individual claim with Bank One in October 2021." But the Bank One settlement reveals that Doe did not merely settle her individual claim but also settled on behalf of 84 other claimants. (Hiller Decl., Ex. C (pg. 58.) The Bank Two settlement also refers to "plaintiffs." (Hiller Decl., Ex. D (pg. 70.) Doe therefore fails to show that "ATTORNEYS represent[ed] CLIENT on an individual basis only" and the 35% contingency fee is applicable. Doe also fails to explain why she would be entitled to the entire remainder of the settlement even if the 35% contingency fee were appropriate. The record begs the question whether the claimants, who apparently executed the Bank One settlement and release, are necessary parties to this action. (Hiller Decl., Ex. C (pg. 58, par. 2).)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)

Off Calendar Aug-22-2024 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF ERIN BRINKMAN, LIEFF, CABRASER, HEIMANN, & BERNSTEIN, LLP AND ANNE SHAVER's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Plaintiffs' "motion for summary judgment" is off calendar.

On April 2, 2024, the parties entered into a stipulation for a briefing schedule regarding their respective motions for summary adjudication. The parties agreed that plaintiffs' motion for summary adjudication could address defendant/cross-complainant's claims for declaratory relief and violation of Business & Professions Code section 17200. On June 21, 2024, plaintiffs filed a "motion for summary judgment" that seeks to dispose of cross-complainant's entire cross complaint. The motion is off calendar because plaintiffs failed to comply with the parties' stipulation. In addition, a court cannot entertain a motion summary adjudication where the only motion noticed for hearing is for summary judgment. (Maryland Cas. Co. v. Reeder (1990) 221 Cal.App.3rd 961, 974 fn. 4; see also Jimenez v. Protective Life Ins. Co. (1992) 8 Cal.App.4th 528, 534-535 [same rule when motion heard by stipulation].)

Appearances:

David McMonigle, Esq., in court, (415) 438-4555, for Plaintiff.

David Hiller, Esq., via Zoom, (415) 967-1984, for Defendant.

Defendant JANE DOE's DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

Ruling - A R G U E D. Upon conclusion of argument, the court adopts the following tentative ruling: Defendant's demurrer to the complaint for declaratory judgment is overruled.Plaintiffs are attorneys who represented defendant in discrimination litigation that ended with her cashing two settlement checks. Later, however, defendant has argued she was not paid enough. The attorney-client relationship was governed by the attorney representation agreement (ARA) attached to the complaint. Most of defendant's demurrer arguments are based on her notion that the ARA had to be modified for plaintiffs' interpretation of it to prevail. Plaintiffs, on the other hand, insist no modification was needed or occurred. If, as here, a contract is attached to a complaint, plaintiff's interpretation must be accepted as correct on a general demurrer. (Aragon-Haas v. Family Security Ins. Services, Inc. (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239.) This rule is close kin to another: no matter how unlikely or improbable, plaintiff's allegations must be accepted as true for purposes of ruling on a demurrer. (Del E. Webb Corp. v. Structural Materials Corp. (1981) 123 Cal.App.3d 593, 604.) In short, it is conceivable defendant could win this case, but not at the pleading stage. Defendant's other argument is that the complaint is uncertain. It is not, and this demurrer ground is disfavored. Any ambiguity "can be clarified under modern discovery procedures." (Khoury v. Maly's of California, Inc. 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 616.)
